# Barbrook-Johnson & Penn, Systems Mapping — seven methods for causal models of systems (deep reference)

**Source:** Barbrook-Johnson, P., & Penn, A. S. (2022). *Systems Mapping: How to build and use causal models of systems*. Palgrave Macmillan / Springer Nature, Cham. ISBN 978-3-031-01833-6 (print); 978-3-031-01919-7 (eBook). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-01919-7. 197 pages. Licence: **CC BY 4.0** (Open Access, "use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit").
**Scope:** open
**Structure:** Twelve chapters. Ch 1 *Introduction* frames systems mapping and positions the seven methods on three conceptual spaces (system-vs-intervention focus × qualitative-vs-quantitative; emphasis-on-participation × intuitive-vs-formal; outputs and analysis Venn). Chs 2–8 are method chapters (Rich Pictures, Theory of Change, Causal Loop Diagrams, Participatory Systems Mapping, Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping, Bayesian Belief Networks, System Dynamics) — each using a near-identical six-section structure: what is it / how to do it / common issues and tricks / good and bad at / brief history / getting started. Ch 9 *What Data and Evidence Can You Build System Maps From?* covers participatory, qualitative, evidence-review, and quantitative modes. Ch 10 *Running Systems Mapping Workshops* is a workshop runbook (planning, venue/materials/tech, facilitation, recording, common issues, online workshops). Ch 11 *Comparing, Choosing, and Combining Systems Mapping Methods* delivers the cross-method comparison table, the choice triangle, the appropriateness-by-property table, the sequential-combinations diagram, and the hybridised-elements table. Ch 12 *Conclusion* offers five take-home messages.
**Citation style:** `(Ch N, "Section name")` for chapter sections, `(Ch N, Fig N.M)` and `(Ch N, Table N.M)` for figures and tables. Page anchors were lost in pymupdf4llm conversion; section names from the converted markdown are used in their place.
**Coverage:** all twelve chapters and every method-chapter sub-section were read in full from the converted markdown (9,124 lines). Figures and tables are referenced by their captions; pymupdf4llm does not extract image content as bitmap, so the visual content of figures (e.g., the obesity CLD, the Maldives stock-and-flow model) is summarised from the figure captions and the surrounding prose. Image classification was deferred (text-only ingest).

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

The authors' core argument is that *systems mapping* is a useful, intuitive, and increasingly demanded family of methods for building causal models of complex systems, but the methodological landscape is muddled, terminology is inconsistent, and practitioners often miss large differences between methods because the methods are subsumed under broad systems-thinking framing [AR] (Ch 1, "Why Did We Write This Book and Who Is It For?"). The book's purpose is to "make the landscape of methods clearer, to help people find, understand, and use these methods more easily" [V] (Ch 1, "Introduction"). They explicitly limit scope to seven methods that "represent causality and influence in a system, and methods which can be used in a participatory way" [V] (Ch 1, "What Methods Are Not in This Book?"), excluding non-causal approaches (mind mapping, stakeholder mapping, giga-mapping, viable systems model, social network analysis, path analysis, concept mapping) and excluding broader schools of practice (participatory action research, design thinking, permaculture) on the grounds that those are "covered well elsewhere and they are entire ways of understanding and acting in their own right, not specific mapping methods" [V] (Ch 1, "What Methods Are Not in This Book?").

A second load-bearing claim is that the value of systems mapping is *not primarily forecasting or prediction* but is concentrated across five general-purpose modes: helping us think, helping us orient ourselves, helping us synthesise and connect information, helping us communicate, and helping us extrapolate from assumptions to implications [AR] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?"). The corollary is that for many projects the value is in the *process* of mapping rather than the product (the map itself) — a position the authors return to repeatedly across method chapters and elevate to a final take-home message [AR] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

A third load-bearing claim is that *appropriateness* is the right frame for choosing between methods. "Where a method fits with both the project and the system, we can say it is an 'appropriate' method" [V] (Ch 11, "Comparing Systems Mapping Methods"). The decision is a three-way triangulation between the purpose, needs, and constraints of the project; the characteristics of the system; and the nature of the methods. The authors warn that bias toward familiar methods, instinct ("if we are more of a quantitative or qualitative thinker"), or pressure from funders or clients shapes the decision more than appropriateness, and may even shape "the types of questions, topics, and domains we approach" before method selection is conscious [AR] (Ch 11, "Choosing Systems Mapping Methods").

A fourth load-bearing claim is that *combining methods* — either sequentially (one method feeding another) or as hybrids (one map combining elements of two or more methods) — is normally valuable when time and resources permit, both because it produces holistic coverage and because it is a productive source of methodological innovation [AR] (Ch 11, "Combining Systems Mapping Methods"). The default position is "combining methods in some form will normally be useful or valuable" [V] (Ch 11, "Combining Systems Mapping Methods").

A fifth load-bearing claim runs through the book as a philosophical undercurrent: the appropriate stance for engaging with complex adaptive systems is *participatory, holistic, systemic, and humble*. "We must accept that we cannot force or control the complex adaptive systems we live in and make up, but we can work with them to steer and nurture the change we want" [V] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

## Part I: Framing — what systems mapping is and how it fits together (Ch 1)

### Definitions: system, map, model, network, nodes, edges

The authors decline to fix one definition of "system", noting that "it is possible to define almost anything as a system with enough mental gymnastics" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?"). They cite Williams and Hummelbrunner's (2011) three points of agreement — systems are made up of elements, the links between elements (processes or interrelationships), and a boundary — and Meadows' (2008) extension to include purpose and organisation: a system is an "interconnected set of elements that is coherently organised in a way that achieves something" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?", quoting Meadows 2008, p. 11) [BT]. The authors' working stance is to use "your system" — the system the user is part of, or the system they intend to map for some purpose — rather than to dwell on definition.

For "map" / "model", the authors note that in the systems mapping world "map" is used synonymously with "model" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?"). Their working definition: "a model is a purposeful simplification of some aspect or perception of reality" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?"). They cite Box and Draper (1987): "All models are wrong, but some are useful" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?") [BT].

The three structural primitives shared across (almost) all methods are introduced as: **Network** — "a set of boxes connected by lines"; **Nodes** — the boxes in a network; **Edges** — the connections or arrows between boxes [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?"). "All except one of the methods in this book always have a network of nodes and edges, representing cause and influence between factors in a system, at their core" [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?") — the exception being Rich Pictures.

### The seven methods and how they relate

The seven methods are introduced in alphabetical order with one-paragraph definitions [AP] (Ch 1, "What Systems Mapping Methods Are in This Book?"):

- **Bayesian Belief Networks** — networks of variables representing their conditional dependencies; strict acyclic structure (no feedbacks); maximum two incoming arrows per node typically; output is computed via conditional probabilities.
- **Causal Loop Diagrams** — networks of variables and causal influences focused on feedback loops of different lengths, built around a "core system engine".
- **Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping** — networks of factors and causal connections, "semi-quantitative", suited to participatory contexts, often producing multiple maps to capture diverse mental models.
- **Participatory Systems Mapping** — networks of factors with causal connections, annotated with salient stakeholder information (importance, vulnerability, controllability), large and complex, analysed via network analysis and submap extraction.
- **Rich Pictures** — free-form drawing of a situation or system, no or only gentle prompts, part of Soft Systems Methodology.
- **System Dynamics** — networks of stocks and flows, normally fully specified quantitatively and used to simulate future dynamics.
- **Theory of Change maps** — networks of concepts following a flow from inputs → activities → outputs → outcomes → impacts, built around an intervention.

The methods are positioned across three conceptual spaces [AP] (Ch 1, "How Do These Methods Relate to One Another?"):

- **Fig 1.1** — *system focus ↔ intervention focus* × *qualitative ↔ quantitative*. PSM, CLD, and Rich Pictures cluster on the qualitative-system-focus side; FCM, System Dynamics, BBN cluster on the quantitative side; Theory of Change is intervention-focused.
- **Fig 1.2** — *emphasis on participation* × *intuitive-easy-to-start ↔ formal-harder-to-start*. Positions the methods on those two spectra (the converted-markdown figure layout was fragmented by OCR; the prose-captured placements include PSM and FCM as participation-emphasising, BBN as neutral on participation, and System Dynamics as on the formal end of the second axis).
- **Fig 1.3** — a Venn diagram of *types of outputs and analysis*. Captures which methods produce purely visual maps, network-analysis outputs, dynamic/simulation outputs, or probabilistic outputs.

Table 1.1 catalogues the *other terms* practitioners use for the same methods [AP] (Ch 1, "How Do These Methods Relate to One Another?"). BBN is also called "Bayesian networks, probability networks, dependency models, influence diagrams, directed graphical models, causal probabilistic models, and Theory of Change maps". CLDs are also called "influence diagrams, system maps, sign graphs, Participatory Systems Mapping", and sometimes "System Dynamics models". Theory of Change goes by "programme theory, intervention theory, logic mapping, logic models, results chain, and outcome mapping". The authors describe the terminological mess as an "unfortunate historical quirk" and decline to "fix" it [V] (Ch 1, "What Is Systems Mapping?").

### Methods deliberately excluded

Table 1.2 lists *not in this book* methods with brief reason and pointer: cognitive mapping, causal mapping, comparative causal mapping, concept mapping (Kane & Trochim), giga-mapping, social network analysis, path analysis, viable systems model, log frames / logical frameworks, ParEvo, spray diagram, stakeholder/actor mapping, mind mapping, Cynefin, Cultural-Historical Activity Theory, participatory (action) research, design thinking, permaculture/systems design [AP] (Ch 1, "What Methods Are Not in This Book?"). The criterion for inclusion is "methods which represent causality and influence in a system, and methods which can be used in a participatory way" [V] (Ch 1, "What Systems Mapping Methods Are in This Book?").

### Five general-purpose uses of systems mapping

The five modes of value are framed as applying to "most types of modelling or analysis" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?"):

1. **Helping us think.** "System maps of all types force us to be more specific about our assumptions, beliefs, and understanding of a system" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?"). The most fundamental value — "You basically cannot avoid having it do this to you!" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?").
2. **Helping us orient ourselves.** Maps reveal positions in the system, support quick development of a fuller understanding, help identify who else is affected and should be included [AP] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?").
3. **Helping us synthesise and connect information.** Flexible mapping types bring together different types of data, evidence, and information. Different visualisations, hyperlinking, and map structure can be used to help people return to underlying information [AP] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?").
4. **Helping us communicate.** "The richness and depth of discussion, while maintaining structure and focus, is often a surprise to first-time participants" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?"). The authors flag the *horrendogram* failure mode here: "system maps are sometimes referred to as 'horrendograms', and much worse (!), when they show us the complexity of a system in an unfiltered manner" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?").
5. **Helping us extrapolate from assumptions to implications.** Simulation-capable methods (System Dynamics) let us "look forward". BBNs let us follow conditional dependencies. FCM lets us compute combined effects. PSM lets us visualise causal pathways. The authors are careful to flag that "people sometimes think of systems mapping as more static and are unable to do this" [V] (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?").

### Why now: gateway tools for complex systems thinking

Systems and complexity sciences have produced "highly technical 'black-box' modelling, appealing metaphors and language which don't directly lead to action and are often misapplied, or overwhelming and paralysing complexity" — "serious problems, which many are now seeking to address, including us" [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?"). Systems mapping, particularly causal mapping, is positioned as "particularly useful 'gateway' tools" for capturing "multi-causality, indirect effects, the uncertain boundaries of open systems, feedbacks, and multiple stakeholder perspectives" [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?"). Emergent effects are explicitly named as "other important complex system characteristics" that "need other modelling approaches" — systems mapping is honestly limited [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?").

The authors flag a *headwind*: "the increasingly fast-paced nature of work, research, and policy, and the increasing attention deficit of stakeholders and users. It used to be the case that you could organise a workshop over two days, and muddle your way through more easily, learning and adapting a method as you went. Now, if you are lucky, you get a half day of people's time, and since the pandemic, you may only have people's attendance virtually" [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?"). This shapes the practical bias of the book toward methods that can be done in short workshops.

## Part II: The seven methods (Chs 2–8)

The method chapters share a common shape: what is it / examples / how to do it / common issues and tricks / good and bad at / brief history / getting started. Cross-method, the authors order chapters "roughly in order of the most qualitative through to the most quantitative" [V] (Ch 1, "What's in the Rest of This Book?") — Rich Pictures (most qualitative) → Theory of Change → CLD → PSM → FCM → BBN → System Dynamics (most quantitative).

### Rich Pictures (Ch 2)

**What it is.** "Rich Pictures are a drawing, a picture, of a system or 'situation'" [V] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures?"). Almost always produced in groups in workshop settings on large pieces of paper. Free-form: "very few, if any, prompts are given by facilitators beyond asking them to 'draw the system'" [V] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures?"). When prompts are used they tend to be "'structures', 'processes', 'climate', 'people', 'issues expressed by people', and 'conflict'" [V] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures?"). Participants normally discouraged from using text. The aim is to avoid the constraints other diagram types introduce. "In sum, Rich Pictures are flexible, can contain almost anything, and emphasise letting participants do what they want above all else" [V] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures?").

**Worked examples.** Two NHS-related Rich Pictures from Bell and Morse (2013a) are presented — an "archetypal-if-poor" Rich Picture of the UK NHS (Fig 2.1) and a Rich Picture of the influence of indicators on sustainable development in Slovakia (Fig 2.2) [AE] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures?").

**How to do it.** Three stages: *planning* (deciding who to invite, structure of sessions, pre-workshop work on focus/system definition); *workshop* (typically 30 minutes minimum to two hours; up to twenty participants per facilitator, smaller groups of three to six drawing one picture; minimal facilitator role; reporting back to whole group); *analysis* (narrative and thematic analysis, aesthetic analysis, or structured content analysis — see Bell, Berg & Morse 2016b for content analysis tools) [AP] (Ch 2, "How Do You Do Rich Pictures?").

**Common issues.** Three named: scepticism that drawing is "childish"; power dynamics or dominant individuals shaping the picture; difficulty developing rich analyses of the produced pictures [AP] (Ch 2, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). *Research fatigue* is a fourth named issue, particularly acute with Rich Pictures because the method's openness lets participants express fatigue more quickly [AP] (Ch 2, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Tricks.** Use icebreakers (the canonical example: "ask them to draw themselves first"); give power to the participants (leave the room during drawing); don't try to force-fix issues during a session [AP] (Ch 2, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: flexibility, openness, ability to capture different perspectives/values/perceptions, low barrier to participation, "even in a tricky process... there is still something to work with" [V] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures Good and Bad At?"). Weaknesses: "rather just things Rich Pictures will never do because it prizes freedom and expression so highly" — won't formalise knowledge precisely, won't provide direct inputs into more formal modelling, cannot directly seed a CLD [AP] (Ch 2, "What Are Rich Pictures Good and Bad At?").

**History.** Rich Pictures emerged from Peter Checkland's Soft Systems Methodology (SSM) at Lancaster University in the 1970s as a way to explore decisions in complex "situations" (Checkland preferred "situations" to "systems" — "reflecting less emphasis on attempting to describe whole systems") [AP] (Ch 2, "A Brief History of Rich Pictures"). The Checkland/Scholes 1990 update and Checkland's 2000 retrospective are flagged as key texts. The 1995–2016 work of Bell & Morse is positioned as "arguably most influential in advocating for thinking of, and using, Rich Pictures as a standalone research method" [V] (Ch 2, "A Brief History of Rich Pictures"). The CATWOE analysis tool is named alongside Rich Pictures as one of SSM's two main innovations: "customers, actors, transformation, worldview, owner, and environment" [V] (Ch 2, "A Brief History of Rich Pictures").

### Theory of Change Diagrams (Ch 3)

**What it is.** "ToC is more than 'just' an individual system mapping method; it is also an entire approach in its own right to framing, structuring, and implementing the design and evaluation of interventions" [V] (Ch 3, "Theory of Change Diagrams"). ToC has roots in the evaluation community — the discipline "focused on assessing the impact and success of interventions" — and has spread to intervention design. "In some domains... if you can't quickly explain your ToC, you will often be thought of as ill-prepared or naïve" [V] (Ch 3, "Theory of Change Diagrams").

ToC diagrams can be textual (as short as one sentence, "don't tend to be much longer than half a page or so") or graphical — "from simple flow diagram-type images, with maybe only a handful of boxes and one or two connections, through to large and complex diagrams" [V] (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?"). Common categories used as the structural columns of a ToC: *inputs* (resources broadly defined), *activities* (actions, events, undertakings), *outputs* (immediate tangible products, akin to project deliverables), *outcomes* (potential short and medium-term effects), *impacts* (long-term effects) [AP] (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?", citing Rogers 2014's "results chain").

**Worked examples.** Two are presented from Vogel & Stephenson (2012) and DSD/SASSA/UNICEF (2012) — a child support grant programme in South Africa (Fig 3.1, top-to-bottom layout, ToC of medium complexity) and an education improvement programme in Ethiopia (Fig 3.2, left-to-right layout, simpler diagram) [AE] (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?").

**Important framing.** "Though the clue is in the name, it is important to keep in mind that ToCs are theories, they represent the mental models of the people who constructed them. They are not maps of reality and they may contain gaps and ambiguities, as indeed may the theories they represent" [V] (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?"). A ToC "should ideally be iterated multiple times, being refined each time" [V] (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?").

**How to create.** Steps: start with the intervention; define long-term impacts; fill in the gaps (the authors recommend starting at the short and medium-term outcomes close to long-term impacts and "working backwards"); make it specific and realistic (always ask who, how much, how); surface the assumptions; consider "theories of no change" or "theories of negative change"; capture feedbacks and interactions; capture disagreement; use it, then iterate [AP] (Ch 3, "How Do You Create Theory of Change Diagrams?").

**Common issues.** Two highlighted: "the multiple pressures to create a narrow or overly simple ToC", and "the tendency of people (stakeholders, or clients if you have them) to treat a ToC diagram as a product rather than a process" [V] (Ch 3, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). The authors flag the *two-version* trick — keep "the 'real one' and a simplified one, which you can use when you cannot resist the pressure to simplify" [V] (Ch 3, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Tricks.** Always ask "how, who, how many, why?"; question the scale of impacts people describe; use text and annotation around the diagram; make workshops fun [AP] (Ch 3, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: representing stakeholders' mental models of how interventions lead to outcomes; framing/structuring evaluation and design; surfacing assumptions; facilitating discussion and self-reflection; revealing gaps in evaluation coverage. Weaknesses: "they can prevent us from taking a whole-system view"; reinforce innate optimism; "may be impossible for them to see their position in a system, or their reality, reflected in a ToC diagram" for those not invested in the intervention [AP] (Ch 3, "What Are Theory of Change Diagrams Good and Bad At?").

**History.** "The birth of ToC is often attributed to the US-based Aspen Institute and its roundtable event in 1995" [V] (Ch 3, "A Brief History of Theory of Change"). Carol Weiss "coined the term 'Theory of Change' to refer to the description of all the steps that lead to the long-term goals of interventions (Weiss, 1995)" [V] (Ch 3, "A Brief History of Theory of Change"). The motivating concerns at the time were (i) inappropriate experimental evaluation methods for large poverty-reduction programmes and (ii) stakeholders' lack of clarity on how interventions create change. ToC has been most adopted in international development, philanthropy, and the third sector. Criticisms are "often around ToC being too linear and not considering wider contexts (Wilkinson et al., 2021)" [V] (Ch 3, "A Brief History of Theory of Change") — a paper to which the authors are a co-author. Van Ongevalle et al. (2014) is cited for actor-focused ToC variants; Davies (2018) for technical-challenges reflection.

### Causal Loop Diagrams (Ch 4)

**What it is.** "CLDs represent a system in three basic elements: boxes, connections, and feedback loops" [V] (Ch 4, "What Is a Causal Loop Diagram?"). Variables in boxes; positive or negative causal influence on connections; "the third element is what makes CLDs more unique" — feedback loops, "both in the construction of the map and in its visualisation" [V] (Ch 4, "What Is a Causal Loop Diagram?"). Loops are "usually focused around a 'core system engine'" — a set of nodes at the centre of the system, often visualised more prominently [V] (Ch 4, "What Is a Causal Loop Diagram?"). R/B annotations label reinforcing and balancing loops. CLDs sit "roughly in the middle of the qualitative-quantitative spectrum of systems mapping methods, perhaps more towards the qualitative side, but hints at dynamics in a system" [V] (Ch 4, "Causal Loop Diagrams").

**Worked examples.** Fig 4.1 — a CLD of a social enterprise's tensions between business activity and social action (Moizer & Tracey, 2010), with three feedback loops (B1, R1, R2) emphasised, double-bar (//) symbols indicating time delay [AE] (Ch 4, "What Is a Causal Loop Diagram?"). Fig 4.2 — the famous UK obesity CLD produced by ShiftN for the UK Government Office for Science (2007), "one of the most high-profile pieces of systems mapping work ever done"; also often "derided or held up as an example of a 'horrendogram' (Penn, 2018); indicative of a form of visualisation which renders problems intractable and overwhelming" [V] (Ch 4, "Causal Loop Diagrams").

**How to create.** Seven steps: collect data and/or evidence; screen for variables and connections and create catalogue; hypothesise the structure and content of the core system engine; develop first diagram with core system engine and twenty nodes; verify and amend; expand model as needed; analysis and use [AP] (Ch 4, "How Do You Create Causal Loop Diagrams?"). Variables should be "expressed clearly as things that can go up or down and should be precise" [V] (Ch 4, "How Do You Create Causal Loop Diagrams?"); avoid *container concepts* like "Technology"; add intermediate variables to resolve unclear connections.

**System archetypes (Kim, 2000).** The eight named archetypes "outline common and intuitive types of core system engines": *drifting goals*, *escalation*, *fixes that fail*, *growth and under-investment*, *limits to success*, *shifting the burden*, *success to the successful*, and *tragedy of the commons* [V] (Ch 4, "How Do You Create Causal Loop Diagrams?", citing Kim 2000) [BT].

**Common issues.** Feedback loops require "a different way of thinking, and perhaps a little more care" [V] (Ch 4, "Commons Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). The "easiest ways to miss one of the key values of CLDs" is to ignore or skip over the loops. Turning the dynamical view into something "usable or 'actionable'" can be difficult — "problem-solving cultures in organisations can get in the way if they revolve around instrumental and 'button-pushing' mindsets" [V] (Ch 4, "Commons Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). "Disrupting a 'vicious cycle' or encouraging a 'virtuous' one is probably the most intuitive way in which we can think about interaction with system dynamics" [V] (Ch 4, "Commons Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Tricks.** Tune the complexity of a map to the visual literacy of your audience; "allocate half of your resources to design and communication" [V] (Ch 4, "Commons Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'") — a striking quantitative recommendation.

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: produces a system map "that is often visually appealing, that hints strongly at a system's dynamics, but that is still flexible, inclusive, and relatively easy to use"; clear stepping-stone to System Dynamics. Weaknesses: can be restrictive with focus on feedbacks; puts a lot of power in the researcher's hands; "if feedback loops are not present, or important, in your system, then the method will be less useful"; no quantitative analysis [AP] (Ch 4, "What Are Causal Loop Diagrams Good and Bad At?").

**History.** CLDs' "early history is intertwined with that of System Dynamics" [V] (Ch 4, "A Brief History of Causal Loop Diagrams"). Jay Forrester used flow diagrams as a mid-point but not CLDs as such. Goodman (1975) "is one of the earliest texts to formally outline CLDs". Simple CLDs were used in the 1972 *Limits to Growth* report — "likely to have raised awareness of them" [AP] (Ch 4, "A Brief History of Causal Loop Diagrams"). "Originally used to communicate the design of a System Dynamics model" but now "more common to use them as a stepping-stone towards a model". Minor debate has existed over whether links should be labelled `+`/`-` or `s`/`o` ("same direction" / "opposite direction").

### Participatory Systems Mapping (Ch 5)

**Author disclosure.** "PSM has evolved a lot over the last ten years... Our experiences over the last ten years, as we have developed Participatory Systems Mapping (PSM), are one of the core motivations for this book" [V] (Ch 5, "Participatory Systems Mapping"). The authors explicitly note this chapter is their method, and they offer the disclosure that "we have experienced all sorts of reactions when using PSM, from the positive and enthusiastic, through to negative and harsh criticism" [V] (Ch 5, "Participatory Systems Mapping"). The core idea: "to turn potentially overwhelming complexity (in systems, and in our models of them), into something more actionable, practical, and usable" [V] (Ch 5, "Participatory Systems Mapping").

**What it is.** "PSM maps are causal models of a system, represented by a network of factors and their causal relations. They are almost always annotated and layered with information about the factors and their connections. Technically speaking, they are directed cyclic graphs, that is, the connections between factors have arrows and there can be feedback loops in the network" [V] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?"). Nodes are called *factors*; specialised factor types include outcomes, functions, and interventions. Connections are positive, negative, or "uncertain or complex" (when relationships depend on other factors/contexts or are strongly nonlinear) [AP] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?").

**Map scale.** "Aiming to build reasonably large networks with at least 20 nodes, often more like 50 or 100. The number of connections is then usually several times larger, running into several hundred on some occasions" [V] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?"). The only hard constraint on size is time.

**Submap-based analysis.** "At the core of the approach to the analysis is the idea of creating submaps... which we can use to focus in on particular questions or issues" [V] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?"). Submaps offer "a way in, to what can be an overwhelmingly large diagram". Starting points for submaps are either *stakeholder-suggested* (what stakeholders flag as important, intervention-relevant, or vulnerable) or *system-suggested* (factors flagged by network-analysis measures — high centrality, bridging structure).

**Submap rules.** Once a starting point is chosen, generate the submap using one or a combination of: one/two/three steps upstream or downstream; ego networks (any nodes and edges connected to the starting factor, and the edges between them); paths between multiple factors of interest. These can be combined via unions or intersections (Fig 5.2 shows the visual logic; Table 5.1 catalogues analysis starting points) [AP] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?").

**Worked example.** Fig 5.1 — a Participatory System Map of the water and agricultural system in a north-east-England river catchment, based on Bromwich et al. (2020) for Defra. Green nodes are system functions; blue nodes are policies; green arrows are positive causal connections; red are negative; blue are complex or unclear [AE] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?").

**How to do PSM.** Nine basic stages: decide on aim of project; define system/boundary; choose stakeholders; design the process; choose focal factor(s) — "usually outcomes or 'functions' of the system... and are the first nodes laid down in the construction of the map"; brainstorm general factors; build the map (start from focal factors, layer policies in only at the end "to prevent using familiar, but perhaps inaccurate, linear models of change"); collect factor and connection information (controllability, vulnerability, ownership); verify and review; design analysis [AP] (Ch 5, "How Do You Do Participatory Systems Mapping?").

**Most common confusion.** "The most fundamental and most common confusion people have about the 'rules of the game' for building a PSM map is what 'positive' and (especially) 'negative' connections are. Sometimes people think of connections in a normative way" — "this factor is good for that factor" rather than "they move together mathematically" [V] (Ch 5, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). The same confusion is named for FCM.

**Tricks.** Be clear about the rules of the game from the start; always ask people to explain; encourage people to take control; keep the whole in mind whilst working on details (*zoom in and out cognitively*); do not put pressure on the process to finish soon (PSM "is inherently an iterative process. The map will change and be refined and may never feel finished"); introduce analysis early [AP] (Ch 5, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: sits between flexible-qualitative and formal-quantitative; offers formalism in data-poor contexts; aimed at finding solutions to "the common critique of system maps as 'horrendograms' but doing this without compromising on depth" [V] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping Good and Bad At?"). Weaknesses: not the right tool at the extremes of the spectrum; "probably its single biggest weakness is its inability to explore dynamics in systems; it is a relatively static method" [V] (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping Good and Bad At?").

**History.** Started with Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping work in Penn et al. (2013). The authors had concerns about FCM analysis being "sensitive to many subtle assumptions" — specifically the threshold/squashing functions and their effect on outputs. "Assumptions made by the researcher often had a greater impact on results than stakeholder input" [V] (Ch 5, "A Brief History of Participatory Systems Mapping"). The idea of network analysis and *causal flow* emerged in Penn et al. (2016); refined through subsequent public-sector projects.

**Software (Table 5.2).** Three categories: general-purpose diagramming (digarams.net, Visio, Conceptboard, Miro — easy to use but no automated map analysis); purpose-built (CECAN's PRSM, Kumu, yED — some analysis available, easy to use); network visualisation and analysis (Gephi, R and Python packages — full analysis, but steep learning curve, layouts generated algorithmically, manual manipulation difficult, not collaborative) [AP] (Ch 5, "Getting Started with Participatory Systems Mapping Yourself").

### Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping (Ch 6)

**Author disclosure.** "FCM holds a special place in our hearts, being one of the first system mapping methods we used together" [V] (Ch 6, "Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping"). The authors note that their concerns about FCM analysis sensitivity led to PSM's development — but they present both schools of FCM thought fairly here.

**What it is.** FCM "involves building a model of a system made up of boxes and connections. The boxes, normally referred to as 'factors' or 'concepts', can represent anything which is expressed as a variable" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"). Importantly, "it does not have to be quantifiable or to have data behind it which means it can be used to capture knowledge without empirical data" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"). Edges represent direct causal influence, not "a vague notion of 'something is going on here'" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?").

**Two analysis schools.** Following Helfgott et al. (2015), the authors distinguish *causal* and *dynamical* approaches (Table 6.1) [BT] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"):

- *Causal approach* — "the strength of links between factors represents how certain or not we are that one factor causes, or suppresses, another. Values of factors produced in analysis represent how strongly caused (or 'activated') they are by changes in other factors based on our level of uncertainty about whether causal links in a map exist or not" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"). Aims to answer "if we change something in the system, what implications does it have for whether the other factors in the system change?" [V]. Threshold/squashing function required to keep values in 0..1 or -1..1.

- *Dynamical approach* — "considers the propagation of effects of one factor on another, producing a simple dynamical representation of the relative magnitude of changes in factor values" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"). Useful to "gauge how relative changes in factor values might play out dynamically, for example, whether change accelerates, stabilises, or dies away" [V]. No thresholding function required.

The four typical output patterns of dynamic analysis: "(i) the factor values all change and stabilise at new values; (ii) the factor values change and continue in one direction in a 'runaway change'-type scenario; (iii) values change but return to zero; or (iv) values change up and down in cycles" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?").

**Critical pitfall.** "When using this mathematical formulation, changing the form of the threshold function can have more of an impact on the results than the map structure, even to the degree of reversing model results. It is crucial therefore to do extensive sensitivity analysis when using a causal approach" [V] (Ch 6, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). This is the analytical concern that prompted the authors to develop PSM.

**Worked examples.** Fig 6.1 — Kok (2009)'s FCM of deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon, developed for dynamical analysis. Fig 6.2 — Penn et al.'s (2013) FCM of the UK Humber region bio-based economy. Drivers (factors with no incoming connections) "are here given a self-reinforcing loop to prevent their value going to zero during analysis" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?"). Fig 6.3 — example outputs from Kok's Amazon FCM, showing four policy-scenario plots.

**How to do FCM.** Three stages: develop list of factors to include (keep to about twenty); construct the map (specify direction, sign, strength of each edge — collect actual strength values at the end, after all connections drawn, so they can be ranked relative to each other); conducting and interpreting analysis [AP] (Ch 6, "How Do You Do Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?").

**Three map-building modes.** "Researchers can build them themselves on whatever evidence or input they deem appropriate; multiple maps can be built in individual interviews with stakeholders, and then combined; or one map can be built in a workshop setting with multiple stakeholders" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?").

**Common issues.** Over-interpretation — "The analysis can seem like magic (especially when done live in a workshop) and offer a false sense of certainty, truth, and scientific rigour" [V] (Ch 6, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). Positive/negative confusion (same as in PSM). The factor-timescale problem — "if factor A influences factor B over many years, and factor C also influences B, but on a daily basis, the analysis of the map will treat them in the same way" [V] (Ch 6, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'") — the solution is normally to redefine the boundary so timescales align.

**Tricks.** Think about the analysis right from the start of map building; look for feedbacks (especially short loops); avoid "boring dynamics" — "ensure these [key outcomes] are not represented as sinks (i.e. only with incoming arrows)" [V] (Ch 6, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: intuitive and quick descriptions; doesn't require data; "a form of quantitative storytelling" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping Good and Bad At?"); ideal for workshop contexts; inclusive of both qualitative and quantitative content; dynamical analysis is "engaging and exciting to see". Weaknesses: "possibility of the analysis being easily misapplied or misunderstood. At worst, meaningless, but quantitative, analysis could be taken as truth" [V] (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping Good and Bad At?").

**History.** FCM "first appeared with Bart Kosko's (1986) paper outlining a modified approach to cognitive maps (themselves first outlined by Axelrod, 1976), applying fuzzy causal functions to connections" [V] (Ch 6, "A Brief History of Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping"). Cognitive maps had binary values for connections; Kosko added (i) fuzzy values for nuanced representation and (ii) the ability to "compute" maps numerically [AP] (Ch 6, "A Brief History of Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping"). Early FCM work was technical and slow to spread; since 2000 FCM has spread "into domain journals and a variety of fields". Two parallel literatures: participatory FCM (maps as discussion/learning tools) and computational FCM (maps as a form of neural network for inference from data). Jetter & Kok (2014) is the recommended detailed history.

**Software.** Diagramming, spreadsheet (manual implementation of dynamic analysis), FCMapper, Mental Modeller (browser-based, causal approach), and R packages [AP] (Ch 6, "How Do You Do Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?").

### Bayesian Belief Networks (Ch 7)

**What it is.** BBNs "are made up of nodes (which here, represent variables, factors, or outcomes in a system) and edges (which represent the causal relations between these nodes)" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). "What sets BBNs apart is that each node has some defined states (e.g. on or off, high or low, present or not present) and some associated likelihoods of being in each of those states. These likelihoods are based, in probabilistic fashion, on the states of the nodes that they are connected to" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). Nodes are "conditionally dependent" on the states of nodes they have a causal relationship with. BBNs are *acyclic* — no cycles or feedbacks, arrows flow in one direction.

**Constraints.** "First, they are acyclic; that is, there cannot be any feedback loops, of any length, in the network. This constraint is imposed for the calculations to work. In complex systems, it is rare for there to be no feedback loops" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). "It is possible to partially represent feedback loops by including multiple nodes for the same thing, but for different time points" — *dynamic BBNs*. "A second constraint... is that most nodes cannot have more than two or three parent nodes (i.e. incoming connections) and nodes should not have more than a handful of states. This constraint is normally imposed so that the conditional probability tables... do not become unworkably large" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?").

**Capturing wider context.** A "typically misunderstood, or simply missed, nuance" — non-zero probabilities in the conditional probability table represent "'everything else going on in the system'" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). Table 7.2 — a simple hypothetical conditional probability table for two interventions and an outcome — shows a 0.1 probability the outcome does not happen even when both interventions are "on", and 0.2 probability the outcome happens when neither is "on". This is the wiggle room BBNs offer for context not formally in the network.

**Bayesian framing.** BBNs are "Bayesian" because of the underlying logic of Bayesian statistics — "probability expressing an expectation of likelihood based on prior knowledge or on a personal belief" — not because Thomas Bayes developed them [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). Parent nodes hold the *prior* information; child nodes' state-updates use Bayesian inference; "the word 'belief' was used in the name of the method right from the start (though not consistently) because this word is used to describe assertions about probabilities in Bayesian statistics" — *not* because BBNs are used in subjective stakeholder processes, "though this is a sometimes useful coincidence" [V] (Ch 7, "A Brief History of Bayesian Belief Networks").

**Two main types of insight.** "(i) assessing the probability of achieving outcomes, and (ii) quantifying the impacts on outcomes of changes elsewhere in the system" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?"). Setting root-node states and looking down; or setting leaf-node observed states and looking up.

**Worked example.** Fig 7.1 — a simple BBN of effects of rainfall and forest cover on river-catchment outcomes (angling potential, farmer income), from Bromley (2005). Fig 7.2 — same BBN with node states and probability distributions visible. Table 7.1 — example conditional probability table for "reservoir storage" given the parent "river flow". Fig 7.3 — dynamic BBN with two time-period instances of "wood extraction" capturing a feedback with "wood stored", based on Landuyt et al. (2013).

**How to use.** Five stages: build the network (either generic-first then constrain, or build directly in BBN form — "the MERIT guidelines document (Bromley, 2005) gives a useful and detailed guide to BBN construction"); constrain the network (no feedbacks, max two-three parents per node); define possible node states (exhaustive, exclusive, normally discrete); elicit conditional probabilities (using probability tables for each node); analysis [AP] (Ch 7, "How Do You Use Bayesian Belief Networks?"). The authors recommend the "build generic, refine later" approach.

**Common issues.** Over-interpretation of precision/reliability of outputs (common to all quantitative methods); the "BBN with conditional probabilities annotated" appearing as a *product* rather than a process; the building process being "demanding for stakeholders" — eliciting conditional probabilities "can create rather repetitive conversations" [V] (Ch 7, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'"). Common pre-emptive critiques from the systems and complexity communities — "the constraints BBN introduce" — are usually "not fatal, and often are unfair" [V].

**Tricks.** Emphasise iteration and learning from the process; build a generic map with stakeholders and refine later; tackle criticism of constraints head-on [AP] (Ch 7, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: probabilistic representations; "quite difficult to produce meaningless analysis with BBN because it is based on extrapolating individual conditional probabilities (which are easy to define in sensible ways)" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks Good and Bad At?"); "transparency... Everything that has gone into the model is clearly visible in the network structure and probabilities. There are no 'hidden' decisions taken by a facilitator or modeller on which the outputs might depend crucially. (For example, like parameter choices in Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping or System Dynamics.)" [V] (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks Good and Bad At?"); ability to capture wider context via conditional probabilities; combine different variable types. Weaknesses: "not good at producing whole-system views"; rare to see BBNs with 100+ nodes; awkward at capturing feedbacks even with dynamic BBN extensions; time-consuming to update.

**History.** "The term 'Bayesian network' was first coined by Judea Pearl in his 1988 book, *Probabilistic Reasoning in Intelligent Systems*. Together with Richard Neapolitan's 1989, and very similarly titled book, *Probabilistic Reasoning in Expert Systems*, the field as it is recognized today was born" [V] (Ch 7, "A Brief History of Bayesian Belief Networks"). The lineage runs through 1980s "Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence" workshops and Philip Dawd's 1970s work on probabilistic dependences. Two construction modes from the start: from data or from stakeholder input. The authors recommend supplementary reading on directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) and Pearl's later causal-inference work — particularly "in economics, quantitative social science, and social data science" [V].

**Software (Table 7.3).** AgenaRisk, BayesiaLab, Bayes Server, DPL, HUGIN Expert, GeNIe (free research/teaching version), OpenMarkov (free and open source), Netica-norsys, UnBBayes (free and open source) [AP] (Ch 7, "Getting Started with Bayesian Belief Networks").

### System Dynamics (Ch 8)

**Framing.** "System Dynamics is probably one of the most widely known methods in this book, owing to its use in the Club of Rome commissioned report, *Limits to Growth* (1972)" [V] (Ch 8, "System Dynamics"). The authors flag the dual nature of the method's reception — "on a surface level, System Dynamics models are intuitive and often convincing... it can create a black-box, magic-like quality" but "discerning model observers and users can see the sensitivity of model results to an array of assumptions" [V] (Ch 8, "System Dynamics").

**What it is.** "A computer simulation method for analysis of complex dynamic systems" — that is: implemented on a computer; allows scenario comparison; "normally used to look at systems which are dynamic or moreover have some interesting dynamic properties" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?"). The model is made up of *stocks* (entities that accumulate or deplete over time, taking numerical values), *flows* (rates of change in stocks, usually represented by a differential equation), and the factors which affect flows.

**Crucial framing — not whole-system.** "Despite having 'system' in the name, System Dynamics models tend to be focused on specific dynamical problems rather than whole systems. In practice, this means that models rarely aim to represent a whole system, but rather focus on carefully constrained sub-sets of a system with the relevant dynamics. This nuance in focus is often not understood, with many researchers and practitioners envisaging System Dynamics as taking a whole-systems view, where in fact it is the 'dynamics' that the method emphasises" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").

**Worked example.** Fig 8.1 — a stock-and-flow System Dynamics model of pollution and tourism in the Maldives (Kapmeier & Gonçalves, 2018). Stocks shown as rectangles; flows as straight hollow arrows with hourglass-type valve symbols; factors as floating text with solid curved arrows; B = balancing loop (self-stabilising); R = reinforcing loop (continues increasing/decreasing); dotted lines = influences not included in calibration due to missing data. The model includes differential equations for the "Tourists" stock and for an "Attractiveness of the Maldives" composite — five inputs (tourist numbers, crowding, demand/supply balance of beds, prices, tourist awareness of pollution) combined through decreasing s-shaped logistic functions normalised against reference values [AE] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?"). Fig 8.2 shows number of tourists across multiple policy scenarios — increasing access or capacity "ultimately leads to a crash in tourist numbers as the resulting pollution from high tourist numbers destroys the destination's attractiveness".

**Prediction debate.** "Many scholars contend that as the models are simplifications of reality, and the reality we are modelling is a complex adaptive system with many components and interactions, models will never be able to provide reliable predictions of future states... However, others take an opposing view, seeing prediction as one of the main purposes of models" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?"). The authors take a pluralist position: "purpose drives the model. If our purpose is to predict, and we have designed with this in mind, it is not beyond the reach of methods like System Dynamics, but it is certainly very difficult, and success may well be fleeting" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").

**Stochastic vs deterministic.** "It is often assumed that System Dynamics models are deterministic using the same exact values each time and thus producing the same result every time; however, this is not true. Many models, and indeed very early examples, included stochastic elements" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").

**Soft factors.** "The founding figures of the method were clear that we did not need to revert to using highly quantitative factors with data to back them up. As long as it makes some sense for stocks to be talked about going 'up and down' and we can put some numbers to them and factors in equations without it being completely meaningless or inappropriate, we can make a model work" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?"). But "in practice, people often favour more quantifiable elements and avoid 'soft' (unquantified) factors" because of data and model availability bias. The authors push back: "All complex adaptive systems will have these components, and they will often play important roles in dynamics, so we cannot ignore them simply because they are more difficult to specify".

**Technical activities.** *Parameterisation* (picking values for parameters); *calibration* (picking values to reproduce some target output, e.g. real data); *validation* (assessing how well model reproduces target outputs); *sensitivity analysis* (changing parameters/structural components systematically to see output sensitivity) [AP] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").

**How to do it.** Six steps that "are never actually done neatly in sequential order, rather much iteration and jumping backward and forward is the norm": problem definition (the most difficult); model conceptualisation (commonly via CLD); model formulation (the conversion to stocks/flows is "one of judgement and modelling choices... rather than a simple conversion"); validation and simulation; analysis; interpretation [AP] (Ch 8, "How Do You Do System Dynamics?").

**Participatory variant.** Sometimes called "group model building", "mediated modelling", or "Participatory System Dynamics" [AP] (Ch 8, "How Do You Do System Dynamics?"). The authors emphasise including stakeholders at all stages, "not assume they do not want to get into technical details... ideally, we want stakeholders to critique and attack our models, so we can uncover bad assumptions, misunderstandings, and the gaps that hamper consensus being reached" [V] (Ch 8, "How Do You Do System Dynamics?").

**Common issues.** Struggling to find a dynamical problem/question suited to System Dynamics; finding it "relatively easy to start building System Dynamics models, but much harder to produce 'good' models and develop genuine insights"; the temptation either to "fall in love" with one's model or to "become disillusioned at the difficultly of good modelling and lose interest" [V] (Ch 8, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Tricks.** Rules of thumb for defining the target: avoid trying to model a whole system; look for dynamical sub-parts; ask for advice from more experienced modellers; "think about what the outputs might look like — can you sketch the graphs over time your model might produce?"; iterate aggressively [AP] (Ch 8, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").

**Good and bad at.** Strengths: developing "high-level strategic insights on dynamical questions"; finding counter-intuitive dynamics; "rigorously play out the implications of an intervention or theory"; teaching dynamic thinking; testing "what if?" assumptions [AP] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics Good and Bad At?"). Weaknesses: not useful for operational or micro questions; aggregate-level representation; "requires a relatively large amount of time to conduct compared to other methods in this book"; difficult to include soft and social factors without prior quantitative work.

**History.** The method "appeared in the 1950s, developed by Jay Forrester, who used it to explore the success and failure of businesses and industrial dynamics" [V] (Ch 8, "A Brief History of System Dynamics"). "Forrester used 'hand simulations'... to explore employment dynamics with managers at General Electric. Forrester and his team quickly moved on to modelling on computers" [V] (Ch 8, "A Brief History of System Dynamics"). The method's name causes confusion: "people often assume there are strong connections with systems science and thinking; however, the method did not emerge out of these fields, so the links can sometimes be weaker than we might expect or hope" [V] (Ch 8, "A Brief History of System Dynamics"). Sterman (2018) is cited as flagging that "progress has been held back by the lack of connection to other modelling and research domains" [AP] (Ch 8, "A Brief History of System Dynamics") [BT].

**Software.** NetLogo (free, open source, with SD functionality on top of agent-based modelling), iThink and Stella (commercial, isee systems), Powersim Studio (commercial, some free versions), Vensim (commercial, some free versions). The DYNAMO simulation language ("no longer in use") is mentioned with affection — "this was the language used for many early System Dynamics models, including the Limits to Growth model" [V] (Ch 8, "How Do You Do System Dynamics?"). Loopy (https://ncase.me/loopy/) is recommended as "an extremely easy-to-use and fun browser-based quasi-System Dynamics tool" for beginners [AP] (Ch 8, "Getting Started with System Dynamics").

## Part III: Cross-cutting issues (Chs 9–11)

### What data and evidence can you build system maps from? (Ch 9)

The chapter classifies the inputs to system maps into four overlapping types via Fig 9.1: *participatory* processes, *qualitative data*, *existing evidence*, and *quantitative data* [AP] (Ch 9, "What Data and Evidence Can You Build System Maps From?").

**A defence of participatory mapping in data-poor contexts.** "When working in a genuine complex adaptive system, it is highly unlikely we will have access to the breadth or depth of quantitative data or evidence we need to formally validate every node and edge in a system map" [V] (Ch 9, "Defending the Use of a Participatory Process to Build and Use Your Map"). Reasons evidence is patchy: many important components are social/behavioural/tacit; data collection is "difficult and expensive"; policy contexts demand timely decisions; complex adaptive systems are open and "things are also always changing". The defence: "we still want to think through system interconnections more thoroughly, even without data. The pragmatic response here is to use participatory approaches to illustrate that system effects might indeed be important" [V] (Ch 9, "Defending the Use of a Participatory Process to Build and Use Your Map").

**Boundary objects.** Maps become *boundary objects* "(see Star & Griesemer, 1989) around which stakeholders and researchers can learn" [V] (Ch 9, "Defending the Use of a Participatory Process to Build and Use Your Map") [BT]. Maps and researchers become *interested amateurs* (Dennett 2014; Johnson 2015) — "actors or objects that can be critiqued and improved by participants without the need to offend other stakeholders and their opinions" [V] (Ch 9, "Defending the Use of a Participatory Process to Build and Use Your Map") [BT].

**Qualitative data.** Building maps from interviews/focus groups/transcripts (not from live participation). Advantages: rich, broad, adaptable collection. Disadvantages: heavy researcher judgement; combining contradictory accounts; "it often happens that one interviewee describes something that can give rise to one map, another interviewee gives us something else, and when we combine them we have a new map, which no individual actually described" [V] (Ch 9, "Using Qualitative Data to Build Your Map"). Software options: NVivo R1 (concept maps), Atlas.ti (network structure central to design), MAXQDA (maps as visualisation function), Causal Map (https://causalmap.app/, purpose-built, in development) [AP] (Ch 9, "Using Qualitative Data to Build Your Map").

**Existing evidence.** Maps built from peer-reviewed studies and reputable grey literature, framed as "in effect doing a systematic review or evidence review focused on building a causal description of a system". Advantages: strong evidential support; less researcher judgement than qualitative coding. Disadvantages: coverage gaps, systematic bias in what literature covers. Recommended reading: Petticrew & Roberts (2005) for systematic reviews, Crawford et al. (2015) for rapid evidence reviews, O'Leary et al. (2017) for evidence mapping [AP] (Ch 9, "Using Existing Evidence to Build Your Map").

**Quantitative data.** Two modes. *First mode*: traditional statistical/causal inference between variable pairs (regression, difference-in-differences, structural equation models for cross-sectional, Granger causality for time series, difference-in-differences for panel data). Cunningham (2021) is the recommended introduction. *Second mode*: extracting causal networks directly via *causal discovery* / *network estimation* methods. The conditional-independence intuition is given: a child's height and word knowledge appear related, but age confounds them — they are conditionally independent given age. Methods include correlation thresholding, Granger causality approaches, statistical structure learning (Bayesian networks and structural causal models), inner composition alignment, cross-convergence mapping. Ospina-Forero et al. (2020) is recommended as an overview in the SDG context. National-scale data-rich systems are flagged as the most amenable to this approach [AP] (Ch 9, "Using Quantitative Data to Build Your Map").

**Combining types.** "Given enough time and resources, it is hard to think of a reason why we would not want to combine them wherever possible" [V] (Ch 9, "Using Different Types of Data and Evidence in Practice"). The most obvious combination: develop in participatory mode, refine with data/evidence (the BBN pattern). The authors close with a "plea for creativity" — any combination of method and information source is valid "as long as we are aware of the potential gaps and omissions in the information we use, and either address them directly, or adjust our aims and claims accordingly" [V] (Ch 9, "Using Different Types of Data and Evidence in Practice").

### Running systems mapping workshops (Ch 10)

The chapter is a workshop runbook. Style is more bullet-heavy than other chapters — used "like a checklist in your planning" [V] (Ch 10, "Running Systems Mapping Workshops").

**Planning.** Purpose (clear written statement); who to invite ("be compelling in your invite, making clear the value of your work to them"); when to hold it; *time-plan document* — "in fifteen-minute sections... if you have a full day, then planning in thirty- or sixty-minute chunks is sufficient" [AP] (Ch 10, "Planning Workshops"). The plan is for thought-through realism, not rigid execution.

**Venue, materials, technology.** "Go to them, if possible, rather than asking them to come to you"; "the nicest room you can get hold of"; flexible seating (cabaret style, boardroom, or huddle around table); usable walls; whiteboard paper "shiny paper which you can draw on with whiteboard pens, and then erase easily when needed with a tissue"; "we tend to err on the side of low-tech options" — for inclusivity and engagement [AP] (Ch 10, "Venue, Materials, and Technology for Workshops"). For continued sessions, digitise printed maps to A1/A0 and overlay transparent whiteboard paper.

**Facilitating.** Have a clear plan; clear prompts; know the rules of the method; practise (with friends/family); manage group dynamics (watch for dominance, hesitation, off-task drawing); *give away power* (ask participants to draw rather than facilitator drawing); take breaks; work in pairs but not more (otherwise "facilitation can get a bit muddled if there are maybe four or five people who participants perceive as facilitators") [AP] (Ch 10, "Facilitating Discussion").

**Capturing and recording.** Two options: dedicated note-taker (default), or audio/video recording (more accurate but inhibits conversation and creates a transcription burden — "Transcription can easily take five or six times the length of the recording when there are multiple people involved" [V] (Ch 10, "Capturing and Recording Workshops"). Photos of the map throughout the process; "the map at the end will be vital to help you digitise".

**Common issues.** *Difficult power dynamics* (separate workshops if a senior figure dominates); *disagreement between stakeholders* ("unless your role is specifically on reconciliation, we would avoid putting the extra burden on your workshop of playing this role"); *confusion about the method* ("be prepared to explain how the mapping method you are using works in two or three different ways"; the canonical prompt: "OK, so that is because as X increases Y tends to go down?" [V] (Ch 10, "Common Issues"); *disengagement of stakeholders* (pre-empt by setting gentle ground rules, e.g. no laptops); *facilitator burnout* (treat as normal; take time, talk to team) [AP] (Ch 10, "Common Issues").

**Online workshops.** Reflective of pandemic-era practice. Attendance is easier online; interaction and discussion are far harder; "shorter but more sessions" works well; software options are important (the actual call software, and the mapping software — share-screen vs collaborative editing); test software properly; "physical and online mapping create different experiences and maps" (smaller view areas push toward zoomed-in subsection focus); burnout more likely but subtle [AP] (Ch 10, "Running Online Workshops"). Recommended reading: Fowler (2020), Khuri & Reed (2020), Dialogue Matters (2020).

### Comparing, choosing, and combining methods (Ch 11)

**Comparing.** Table 11.1 is a multi-row × multi-column comparison of all seven methods on type of map, level of focus, mode of construction, emphasis on participation, mode of analysis, ease of use, key contributions, key constraints, when most appropriate. The authors flag that "history and roots of each method, and their deeper ontological and epistemological standpoints are missing from this table" — "extremely difficult to meaningfully simplify down into table form" [V] (Ch 11, "Comparing Systems Mapping Methods").

**Choosing — the appropriateness triangle.** "Appropriateness" is the central concept. "It involves triangulating between the purpose, needs, and constraints of your project; the characteristics of the system and context you are working in; and the nature of the methods you are considering using. Where a method fits with both the project and the system, we can say it is an 'appropriate' method" [V] (Ch 11, "Comparing Systems Mapping Methods"). Fig 11.1 visualises this as a three-corner triangle (project / system / methods), inspired by Stern et al. (2012) and Befani (2020).

**Choice biases.** "Powerful forces which might interfere with our decision. We may have bias towards methods we know already, or methods which we are instinctively more comfortable with (e.g. if we are more of a quantitative or qualitative thinker), or methods we think others want us to use (e.g. funders, clients, or colleagues)" [V] (Ch 11, "Comparing Systems Mapping Methods").

**Most appropriate by project/system property.** Table 11.2 sketches which methods are most appropriate given different project and system properties [AP] (Ch 11, "Choosing Systems Mapping Methods"). Befani (2020) is recommended as a more systematic scoring tool.

**Data availability.** "In practice, people often feel that data availability constrains their modelling choices tightly. However, in reality, this is often only to fit into typical or accepted modes of using methods. We believe we should be more open about the way data availability constrains choice, but also be clearer about how that choice will constrain how model results can be interpreted and used" [V] (Ch 11, "Choosing Systems Mapping Methods").

**Combining — two modes.** *Sequential* — one method's outputs feed another (Fig 11.2 shows obvious combinations). *Hybridised* — one map combines elements of two or more methods (Table 11.3 lists "non-exhaustive" hybrid possibilities). The authors note "we have suggested above the possibility of converting (parts of) a PSM into a BBN, which would require constraining the network (i.e. pruning connections so that there is maximum two inputs to a factor and removing any feedbacks)" [V] (Ch 11, "Combining Systems Mapping Methods") — and ask "do we ask stakeholders to do it or do it ourselves? What is the basis for our judgements?" Recommended reading: Voinov et al. (2018) on participatory modelling tool selection; Befani (2020) for choosing evaluation methods; Howick & Ackermann (2011) for mixing Operational Research methods; Barbrook-Johnson & Carrick (2021) for combining complexity-framed methods [AP] (Ch 11, "Getting Started with Choosing and Combining Methods").

## Part IV: Concluding stance (Ch 12)

The conclusion offers five take-home messages [AP] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages"):

1. **See system maps as living documents.** Plan for maintenance, updating, re-use. The point is "the capacity and skills you help create in a stakeholder team to continue the work themselves".

2. **Be clear about whether you want value from process, outputs, or both.** "We find we often expect much more value in process than the stakeholders we work with, who are often surprised just how much they gain from this. The opportunity to reflect and generate understanding with others is surprisingly powerful. Often more so than delivering 'answers'" [V] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

3. **Climb the ladder of using methods.** Three steps: (i) learn one method, apply it to everything ("Maslow's hammer"); (ii) become aware of similar methods and when they should be used instead; (iii) master more methods and "understand the detail and nuance of how each method operates and so can make informed decisions, with stakeholders, about what to use and when".

4. **Decide whether to prioritise participation or conceptual rigour.** "In an ideal world we could do deeply participatory work which also produced models which were completely conceptually and scientifically sound. In practice, this is rarely possible. Stakeholders will want things included which a neutral 'expert' wouldn't" [V] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

5. **Advocate for participatory steering of complex adaptive systems.** "We must accept that we cannot force or control the complex adaptive systems we live in and make up, but we can work with them to steer and nurture the change we want" [V] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

## Key cross-method statistics and facts

| Fact | Source | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Seven methods covered: Rich Pictures, Theory of Change, Causal Loop Diagrams, Participatory Systems Mapping, Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping, Bayesian Belief Networks, System Dynamics | (Ch 1, "What Systems Mapping Methods Are in This Book?") | [V] |
| PSM target size: "at least 20 nodes, often more like 50 or 100" with connections "several times larger" | (Ch 5, "What Is Participatory Systems Mapping?") | [V] |
| CLD target size for the core engine: "Twenty nodes seem to be a useful limit here, beyond this the core becomes too unwieldy" | (Ch 4, "How Do You Create Causal Loop Diagrams?") | [V] |
| FCM map size: "in practice, it is useful to limit the number of factors at this stage to twenty" | (Ch 6, "How Do You Do Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?") | [V] |
| BBN typical size: "we have tended to see networks with no more than roughly twenty nodes" | (Ch 7, "How Do You Use Bayesian Belief Networks?") | [V] |
| BBN node constraint: "most nodes cannot have more than two or three parent nodes" | (Ch 7, "What Are Bayesian Belief Networks?") | [V] |
| Rich Pictures workshop scale: up to 20 participants per facilitator, smaller groups of 3–6 per picture, "whole groups as large as fifty" | (Ch 2, "How Do You Do Rich Pictures?") | [V] |
| Rich Pictures workshop length: "in as little as thirty minutes if needed, but more commonly around two hours" | (Ch 2, "How Do You Do Rich Pictures?") | [V] |
| CLD design-and-communication allocation: "we would suggest thinking about using half of your time or resources on this" | (Ch 4, "Commons Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'") | [V] |
| Transcription burden: "Transcription can easily take five or six times the length of the recording when there are multiple people involved" | (Ch 10, "Capturing and Recording Workshops") | [V] |
| Loops vary by sign and balance pattern: B (balancing, self-stabilising); R (reinforcing, runaway) | (Ch 4, "What Is a Causal Loop Diagram?"; Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?") | [V] |
| Eight system archetypes (Kim 2000): drifting goals, escalation, fixes that fail, growth and under-investment, limits to success, shifting the burden, success to the successful, tragedy of the commons | (Ch 4, "How Do You Create Causal Loop Diagrams?") | [V] |
| Four outputs of FCM dynamic analysis: stabilise at new values; runaway change; return to zero; cycles | (Ch 6, "What Is Fuzzy Cognitive Mapping?") | [V] |
| The five ToC categories (Rogers 2014's "results chain"): inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, impacts | (Ch 3, "What Is Theory of Change Mapping?") | [BT] (cite Rogers 2014) |
| Five general-purpose uses of systems mapping: helping us think, orient, synthesise, communicate, extrapolate | (Ch 1, "How Can Systems Mapping Be Useful?") | [AP] |

## Positions the author explicitly frames against

- **"Black-box" highly technical modelling that does not lead to action.** Named as one of the three failures of historical complexity-science applications [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?").
- **Appealing metaphors and language that do not lead to action.** Named as second failure [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?").
- **Overwhelming and paralysing complexity (the "horrendogram" failure mode).** Named as third failure and recurring concern; central frame for PSM's submap-based response [V] (Ch 1, "Why Think About Systems Mapping Now?"; Ch 5, "Participatory Systems Mapping").
- **Treating ToC diagrams as products rather than processes.** "The 'product' of a ToC process, normally the diagram itself" is critiqued; the value lies as much in process as product [V] (Ch 3, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").
- **Forcing all decisions through severity-of-outcome or canonical category boxes.** Pressures to create overly narrow or simple ToC diagrams resisted by argument [AP] (Ch 3, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").
- **Researcher-assumption-driven FCM analysis where threshold function dominates results.** "Changing the form of the threshold function can have more of an impact on the results than the map structure, even to the degree of reversing model results" — the analytical concern that prompted PSM's development [V] (Ch 6, "Common Issues and 'Tricks of the Trade'").
- **Validation-as-quantitative-truth view of model legitimacy.** "Some researchers... only quantitatively validated models, of any type, are valid or useful" — the framing the authors push back against in the data-and-evidence chapter [V] (Ch 9, "Defending the Use of a Participatory Process to Build and Use Your Map").
- **Outcome-as-prediction view of model purpose.** The pluralist position taken vs strict-predictionists in the System Dynamics chapter [AR] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").
- **The System-Dynamics-as-whole-system view.** "Many researchers and practitioners envisaging System Dynamics as taking a whole-systems view, where in fact it is the 'dynamics' that the method emphasises" [V] (Ch 8, "What Is System Dynamics?").
- **The bias toward methods we already know.** "We may have bias towards methods we know already... methods we think others want us to use" — explicitly named as one of the powerful forces interfering with the appropriateness decision [V] (Ch 11, "Comparing Systems Mapping Methods").
- **The Maslow's-hammer failure mode in method use.** "You have your hammer, and now everything looks like a nail" — the first rung of the ladder of using methods, which the authors urge users to climb beyond [V] (Ch 12, "Our Final Take-Home Messages").

## Citation and source-integrity notes

- **Conversion path.** PDF → markdown via `pymupdf4llm` 0.2.9 (~21 s for 197 pages). Output 407,164 chars / 9,124 lines.
- **Citation style.** `(Ch N, "Section name")` since pymupdf4llm did not preserve page anchors. Section names are taken from the converted markdown's chapter headers and section captions.
- **Coverage.** Full read of all twelve chapters from the converted markdown. The Index (pages 183–186) was read for cross-reference verification of concept names. Acknowledgements, About the Authors, List of Figures, and List of Tables were read as part of Pass A/B but are not the source of any body claims.
- **Figures.** All figures are referenced by caption only — pymupdf4llm does not extract figure content as searchable text. The figure captions and surrounding prose are the source of all figure-related claims.
- **Image scope.** Text-only ingest at operator request. Image classification deferred to a later `ingesting-images` pass.
- **Licence.** CC BY 4.0 (verified at the source — the title-page legal block and every chapter's "Open Access" footer carry the same CC BY 4.0 statement and link). Scope: open. Redistribution is unrestricted with attribution.
